summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically announces the release of Code Complete 2nd Edition, a book he considers essential reading for all developers. He shares his personal practice of owning multiple copies and urges developers to read the top books on his recommended list. The post highlights Steve McConnell's observations about how developers use buzzwords like 'agile' or 'object-oriented' as excuses to skip fundamental practices like requirements and design. Atwood also mentions McConnell's upcoming book on software estimation, humorously dubbed the 'I'm 99% done except for some cleanup!' book.
key_points: Code Complete 2 is Atwood's top recommended book for developers, important enough that he owns multiple copies
Developers frequently use trendy methodologies (OOP, Agile) as excuses to skip fundamental practices like requirements gathering and design
The gap between talking about good practices and actually using them has been a constant in software development for decades
Good programmers invest effort to learn current practices properly; poor programmers just learn the buzzwords
Software estimation remains a 'black art' worthy of its own dedicated book
money_quotes: If I was rich enough to buy a copy of those books for every developer on earth, I would.
There are still far more people who talk about good practices than who actually use good practices.
I see far too many people using current buzzwords as a cloak for sloppy practices.
Most of those people weren't really doing object-oriented programming – they were hacking, and the results were predictable, and poor.
Right now, people are saying 'I don't have to do requirements or design because I'm doing agile development.' Again, the results are easy to predict, and poor.
Good programmers invest the effort to learn how to use current practices. Not-so-good programmers just learn the buzzwords, and that's been a software industry constant for a half century.
key_insight: Buzzwords and methodologies change over time, but the pattern of developers using them as excuses to skip fundamental engineering practices remains constant.
